name: Greek Expanded 2
description: Comprehensive expansion of Greek mythology with additional heroes, monsters, and secondary deities.

beings.name: Jason
beings.summary: Leader of Argonauts who sought Golden Fleece and faced numerous trials. Jason gathered heroes including Heracles and Orpheus to sail on Argo, facing challenges from King Aeetes to obtain the Golden Fleece. He later betrayed Medea, leading to her revenge and his tragic death. Represents heroic leadership and the complex consequences of ambition and broken promises.

beings.name: Achilles
beings.summary: Greatest Greek warrior of Trojan War, invulnerable except for his heel. Achilles was dipped in River Styx as infant, making his body invulnerable except where his mother held him. He dominated Trojan War until Paris shot him in his only vulnerable spot. Represents tragic flaw concept and how even the mightiest heroes have fatal weaknesses.

beings.name: Odysseus
beings.summary: Cunning king of Ithaca who endured ten-year journey home from Trojan War. Odysseus used cleverness to escape Cyclops, resist Circe's magic, and navigate Scylla and Charybdis. His journey tested his wisdom and perseverance through divine trials and human challenges. Represents the triumph of intelligence over brute force and the human cost of prolonged absence.

beings.name: Orpheus
beings.summary: Musician who attempted to rescue his wife Eurydice from underworld with his art. Orpheus' music was so beautiful it charmed gods, humans, and even nature itself. He failed in his quest to bring Eurydice back by looking at her too soon, losing her forever. Represents the power of art to transcend death but also its limitations in the face of ultimate loss.

beings.name: Sirens
beings.summary: Dangerous sea creatures whose enchanting songs lured sailors to their deaths. The Sirens lived on an island and sang irresistible songs that made sailors crash their ships on rocks. Only Odysseus survived their song by having himself tied to mast while crew plugged their ears. Represents the deadly allure of temptation and the need for restraint to survive destructive desires.

beings.name: Lernaean Hydra
beings.summary: Multi-headed serpent monster that grew two heads for each one cut off. The Hydra lived in swamps of Lerna and terrorized the region until Heracles killed it as his second labor. Each severed head multiplied, making the monster seemingly impossible to defeat until Heracles used fire to cauterize the necks. Represents problems that worsen when confronted with force and the need for clever solutions.

beings.name: Cerberus
beings.summary: Three-headed dog guarding the underworld entrance, overcome by Heracles. Cerberus prevented the living from entering and the dead from leaving Hades' realm. Heracles captured the beast as his final labor, demonstrating his mastery over both life and death. Represents the boundary between worlds and the challenge of crossing from mortal to divine existence.

beings.name: Demeter
beings.summary: Goddess of harvest and agriculture who caused winter while searching for Persephone. Demeter's daughter Persephone was abducted by Hades, causing Demeter to neglect the earth and create winter. When Persephone returns for part of year, Demeter brings spring and summer. Represents the cycles of nature and maternal grief that affects the entire world.

beings.name: Hestia
beings.summary: Goddess of the hearth and home who maintained eternal virginity. Hestia tended the sacred fire on Mount Olympus and received first offerings at every household. She gave up her Olympian seat to Dionysus to maintain peace. Represents the stability of home and the sacred importance of domestic fire in ancient Greek life.

beings.name: Nymphs
beings.summary: Nature spirits who inhabit forests, mountains, and waters, often pursued by gods and heroes. Nymphs personify natural beauty and serve as muses, lovers, or guides for mortals. They can live for centuries but are not immortal like gods. Represents the sacredness of natural places and the intersection of divine and mortal worlds.

beings.name: Satyrs
beings.summary: Half-goat, half-human nature spirits who followed Dionysus and loved wine and music. Satyrs have goat legs, horns, and human torsos, embodying untamed forest desires. They play pipes and chase nymphs, representing the wild, lustful aspects of nature that civilization tries to control. They served Dionysus in his processions and celebrations.

beings.name: Three Graces
beings.summary: Goddesses of charm, beauty, and creativity who attended Aphrodite and brought joy. The Graces - Aglaea (splendor), Euphrosyne (mirth), and Thalia (good cheer) - personified the qualities that make life beautiful and worth living. They danced and sang at divine gatherings, spreading grace and happiness. Represents the essential qualities that make human life meaningful and joyful.

beings.name: Three Fates
beings.summary: Three goddesses who determine the length and quality of mortal lives. The Fates - Clotho (spinner), Lachesis (measurer), and Atropos (cutter) - control the thread of life from birth to death. Even Zeus cannot change their decisions. Represents the Greek concept of destiny and the belief that some aspects of life are beyond divine control.

beings.name: Daedalus
beings.summary: Master craftsman and inventor who created Labyrinth and invented wings to escape imprisonment. Daedalus was the greatest artisan of ancient Greece, capable of creating impossible structures and devices. He built the Labyrinth for Minos and later crafted wings for himself and Icarus to escape Crete. Represents human ingenuity and the tragic consequences of overreaching ambition.

beings.name: Icarus
beings.summary: Son of Daedalus who flew too close to sun and fell to his death. Icarus ignored his father's warnings about flying neither too low nor too high, drawn by the beauty of the sun. His wax wings melted, causing him to fall into the sea. Represents the danger of youthful ambition and the consequences of ignoring wise counsel.

beings.name: Ariadne
beings.summary: Princess of Crete who helped Theseus defeat the Minotaur and was later abandoned. Ariadne fell in love with Theseus and gave him a thread to navigate the Labyrinth, saving Athenian youths from sacrifice. Theseus abandoned her on Naxos, but Dionysus found and married her. Represents the vulnerability of women in heroic narratives and divine compensation for human betrayal.

beings.name: Echo
beings.summary: Mountain nymph cursed to only repeat the last words she heard, rejected by Narcissus. Echo was punished by Hera for distracting her while Zeus pursued other women. She fell in love with Narcissus but could only repeat his words, leading to his rejection and her fading away. Represents unrequited love and the pain of being unable to express oneself.

beings.name: Narcissus
beings.summary: Beautiful youth who fell in love with his own reflection and died staring at it. Narcissus was so handsome that everyone who saw him fell in love, but he rejected all advances including Echo's. Nemesis led him to a pool where he saw his reflection and fell in love, dying while gazing at himself. Represents the danger of self-love and the tragedy of being unable to love others.

beings.name: Phineus
beings.summary: King of Argos who sent Jason on quest for Golden Fleece to remove his threat. Phineus usurped the throne from Jason's father and tried to eliminate Jason by sending him on impossible quest. He promised Jason the throne if he returned with Fleece, hoping Jason would die. Represents political ambition and the use of heroes as tools for power.

beings.name: Atlas
beings.summary: Titan condemned to hold up the heavens for eternity after war against Olympians. Atlas fought with Cronus against Zeus and other Olympians in the Titanomachy. As punishment, he must forever bear the weight of the celestial spheres on his shoulders. Represents the burden of eternal punishment and the consequences of opposing divine order.

beings.name: Prometheus
beings.summary: Titan who stole fire from gods and gave it to humanity, suffering eternal punishment. Prometheus created humans from clay and cared for them, stealing divine fire when Zeus withheld it. As punishment, an eagle ate his regenerating liver daily. He represents human progress through divine rebellion and the cost of challenging cosmic authority.

beings.name: Epimetheus
beings.summary: Titan who gave animals to humans but accepted Pandora, bringing evil to world. Epimetheus means 'afterthought' and represents hasty action without foresight. He distributed gifts to animals but had nothing left for humans, leading to Pandora's creation and release of evils. Represents the consequences of poor planning and the introduction of suffering to human existence.

beings.name: Helios
beings.summary: Sun god who drives golden chariot across sky each day, seeing all earthly events. Helios rides his chariot from east to west, illuminating the world and witnessing everything that happens below. His son Phaethon once tried to drive the chariot but crashed, burning parts of earth. Represents cosmic order, the power of the sun, and the danger of mortals attempting divine tasks.

beings.name: Selene
beings.summary: Moon goddess who drives silver chariot across night sky and falls in love with mortal. Selene illuminates the world with gentle moonlight and watches over sleeping mortals. She loved the shepherd Endymion and put him in eternal sleep so she could visit him nightly. Represents the mysterious influence of the moon and the power of divine love that transcends mortal limitations.

beings.name: Eros
beings.summary: God of love and desire who shoots golden arrows to ignite passion in gods and mortals. Eros can make anyone fall desperately in love by striking them with his arrows. He played crucial role in Zeus's numerous affairs and the Trojan War's beginning. Represents the uncontrollable nature of love and its power to overcome reason and divine will.

stories.name: Jason's Quest for the Golden Fleece
stories.summary: Hero assembles Argonauts to seek mythical Golden Fleece and claim his throne.
stories.description: Jason gathers heroes including Heracles, Orpheus, and Theseus to sail to Colchis for the Golden Fleece. With help of Medea's magic, he overcomes dragon-guarded fleece and returns, but later betrays Medea leading to tragedy.

stories.name: Achilles' Heel and the Trojan War
stories.summary: Invulnerable warrior's single weakness leads to his death in the Trojan War.
stories.description: Achilles dominates Trojan War until Paris, guided by Apollo, shoots him in his only vulnerable spot - his heel where his mother held him when dipping him in River Styx. His death marks the turning point in the fall of Troy.

stories.name: Odysseus' Ten-Year Journey Home
stories.summary: Cunning hero's decade-long struggle to return home after Trojan War.
stories.description: Odysseus faces Cyclops, Sirens, Circe's magic, and Scylla/Charybdis during his ten-year journey home. His cleverness and perseverance overcome divine trials, but he returns to find his kingdom in disarray and must fight to reclaim it.

stories.name: Orpheus and Eurydice
stories.summary: Musician's attempt to rescue his wife from underworld through the power of art.
stories.description: Orpheus' music charms Hades and Persephone, allowing him to lead Eurydice from death. He must not look back at her until they reach the living world, but his anxiety makes him glance too soon, losing her forever.

stories.name: Daedalus and Icarus' Flight from Crete
stories.summary: Inventor crafts wings to escape imprisonment, but son ignores warnings and dies.
stories.description: Daedalus creates wax wings for himself and Icarus to escape King Minos' prison. Despite warnings about flying neither too low nor too high, Icarus is drawn to sun's beauty, his wings melt, and he falls to his death.

stories.name: Echo and Narcissus
stories.summary: Nymph cursed to repeat words falls for beautiful youth who loves only himself.
stories.description: Echo falls in love with Narcissus but can only repeat his words. He rejects her and she fades away until only her voice remains. Nemesis then leads Narcissus to see his reflection, and he falls in love with himself, dying while gazing.

stories.name: Prometheus Steals Fire for Humanity
stories.summary: Titan's rebellion against divine order to bring progress to humanity.
stories.description: Prometheus steals fire from Olympus and gives it to humans after Zeus withholds it. As punishment, Zeus chains him to a rock where an eagle daily eats his regenerating liver. His action represents human progress through challenging divine authority.

stories.name: Heracles' Twelve Labors
stories.summary: Hero performs impossible tasks to atone for madness-induced crime.
stories.description: Driven mad by Hera, Heracles kills his family and must perform twelve labors for King Eurystheus. Tasks include killing Nemean Lion, capturing Cerberus, cleaning Augean Stables, and obtaining golden apples - each seemingly impossible but achieved through strength and cleverness.

stories.name: Demeter and Persephone's Seasonal Cycle
stories.summary: Mother goddess causes winter while searching for abducted daughter.
stories.description: Hades abducts Persephone, causing Demeter to neglect earth and create winter. Zeus intervenes, allowing Persephone to spend part of year with her mother, bringing spring and summer when she returns.

stories.name: Phineus Sends Jason on Golden Fleece Quest
stories.summary: Usurper king sends hero on impossible quest to eliminate political threat.
stories.description: Phineus usurped Jason's throne and sends him for Golden Fleece, hoping he'll die. Jason succeeds with hero help, but Phineus refuses to honor his promise, leading to Medea's revenge and eventual restoration of Jason's rule.

places.name: Colchis
places.summary: Distant kingdom where Golden Fleece was guarded by dragon and sleepless dragon.
places.description: Far eastern kingdom ruled by King Aeetes, where the Golden Fleece hung in sacred grove guarded by a sleepless dragon. Jason and Argonauts journeyed here to obtain the fleece, facing numerous challenges including the dragon itself.

places.name: Ithaca
places.summary: Odysseus' island kingdom, symbol of home and the goal of his long journey.
places.description: Small island kingdom ruled by Odysseus, representing the stability of home and family. After twenty years away at war and journey, Odysseus returns to find his kingdom in disarray and must fight to restore order.

places.name: Crete
places.summary: Island kingdom of Minos, site of Labyrinth and Minotaur imprisonment.
places.description: Powerful island kingdom ruled by King Minos, where Daedalus built the Labyrinth to imprison the Minotaur. Later became the place from which Daedalus and Icarus escaped with their wax wings.

places.name: Naxos
places.summary: Island where Theseus abandoned Ariadne, later found by Dionysus.
places.description: Island where Theseus abandoned Ariadne after she helped him defeat the Minotaur. She was discovered here by Dionysus, who married her and provided divine compensation for human betrayal.

places.name: Delphi
places.summary: Sacred site of Apollo's oracle, center of ancient Greek wisdom and prophecy.
places.description: Mountain sanctuary where Apollo spoke through priestess Pythia, providing cryptic prophecies to seekers from all Greek city-states. Considered the center (omphalos) of the world and site of the Pythian Games.

places.name: Mount Olympus
places.summary: Highest mountain in Greece, home of the Olympian gods and center of divine authority.
places.description: Towering mountain where the twelve Olympian gods reside and rule the cosmos. From here Zeus throws thunderbolts, the gods observe mortal affairs, and divine councils determine the fate of worlds.

places.name: Underworld Entrance
places.summary: Gateway to Hades' realm guarded by Cerberus, boundary between life and death.
places.description: Mystical entrance to the underworld where the three-headed dog Cerberus prevents the living from entering and the dead from leaving. Only Heracles successfully passed this boundary in his final labor.

places.name: River Styx
places.summary: Sacred river of the underworld where gods swear unbreakable oaths and Achilles gained invulnerability.
places.description: Underworld river where gods swear binding oaths that even Zeus cannot break. Thetis dipped Achilles in its waters, making him invulnerable except where she held him by the heel.

places.name: Argos
places.summary: Ancient city-state ruled by Phineus, Jason's homeland and starting point of Argonaut quest.
places.description: Prominent Greek city-state where Jason was raised and from which he launched his quest for the Golden Fleece. Later ruled by Phineus who usurped the throne and sent Jason on the dangerous quest.

places.name: Corinth
places.summary: City where Jason and Medea fled after their marriage ended in tragedy.
places.description: Greek city-state where Jason and Medea took refuge after fleeing Colchis. Here their marriage deteriorated, leading Jason to abandon Medea for a political marriage, causing her to murder their children and escape.

motifs.name: Heroic Flaw Leading to Tragedy
motifs.summary: Even greatest heroes possess fatal weaknesses that lead to their downfall.
motifs.description: Pattern where heroes like Achilles (heel), Icarus (ambition), and Jason (betrayal) have specific flaws that cause their destruction despite their greatness. Represents Greek understanding that perfection is impossible and even the mightiest have vulnerabilities.

motifs.name: Divine Retribution for Hubris
motifs.summary: Gods punish mortals who overstep their boundaries through excessive pride.
motifs.description: Pattern where mortals who challenge divine authority or show excessive pride face severe punishment. Icarus flying too high, Niobe boasting about her children, and Arachne challenging Athena all result in divine retribution.

motifs.name: Heroic Quest for Magical Artifacts
motifs.summary: Heroes undertake dangerous journeys to obtain objects of divine power.
motifs.description: Pattern where heroes seek magical objects like the Golden Fleece, Gorgon's head, or golden apples. These quests test the hero's worthiness and often require divine aid, cleverness, and the overcoming of impossible odds.

motifs.name: Transformation as Divine Punishment or Reward
motifs.summary: Gods transform beings into animals, plants, or objects as punishment or blessing.
motifs.description: Pattern where transformation serves as divine intervention - punishment for transgression (Arachne to spider, Daphne to laurel) or reward (Psyche to goddess). Represents fluid boundaries between human, animal, and divine realms in Greek cosmology.

motifs.name: Tragic Unrequited Love with Divine Beings
motifs.summary: Mortals who love gods face impossible obstacles and tragic outcomes.
motifs.description: Pattern where mortals fall in love with gods or divine beings but cannot maintain the relationship. Echo loves Narcissus who only loves himself, Daphne flees Apollo to become a laurel tree, representing the tragic gap between mortal and divine love.

motifs.name: Long Journey and Difficult Homecoming
motifs.summary: Heroes face greater challenges returning home than during their adventures.
motifs.description: Pattern where the journey home proves more difficult than the quest itself. Odysseus faces twenty years of trials to return to Ithaca, Agamemnon returns to be murdered by his wife, representing the challenges of reintegrating after profound change.

motifs.name: Parent-Child Conflict and Generational Struggle
motifs.summary: Generational conflicts where children overthrow or challenge their parents' authority.
motifs.description: Pattern where the younger generation challenges the older - Zeus against Cronus, Heracles against Hera, Apollo against Python. Represents the cyclical nature of power struggles and the inevitable replacement of old order by new forces.

groups.name: Argonauts
groups.summary: Band of heroes who sailed with Jason on quest for Golden Fleece.

groups.name: Olympians
groups.summary: Twelve major deities who rule cosmos from Mount Olympus after defeating Titans.

groups.name: Titan Family
groups.summary: Primeval deities who ruled cosmos before Olympians, led by Cronus.

groups.name: Nine Muses
groups.summary: Goddesses of arts and sciences who inspire human creativity and knowledge.

groups.name: Giants
groups.summary: Primeval beings of immense size and strength who opposed the gods.

objects.name: Golden Fleece
objects.summary: Magical sheep's fleece that symbolizes kingship and divine authority.

objects.name: Argo Ship
objects.summary: Magical ship built by Argus with divine assistance to carry heroes on their quest.

objects.name: Daedalus' Wax Wings
objects.summary: Ingenious but flawed invention that enabled escape but led to tragedy.

objects.name: Achilles' Divine Armor
objects.summary: Magical armor forged by Hephaestus, except for the vulnerable heel.

objects.name: Heracles' Club
objects.summary: Massive club that symbolizes raw strength and heroic power.

objects.name: Orpheus' Lyre
objects.summary: Magical lyre whose music could charm gods, humans, and even nature itself.

objects.name: Prometheus' Sacred Fire
objects.summary: Divine fire stolen for humanity, symbolizing progress and civilization.

sources.title: Bibliotheca
sources.description: Comprehensive collection of Greek myths and heroic legends from the Library of Alexandria.

sources.title: Metamorphoses
sources.description: Poetic collection of transformation myths from Greek mythology, including numerous stories of gods and mortals changing form.

sources.title: Iliad
sources.description: Epic poem describing the Trojan War and the deeds of Greek heroes, particularly Achilles.

sources.title: Odyssey
sources.description: Epic poem describing Odysseus' ten-year journey home after the Trojan War, featuring encounters with monsters, gods, and magical beings.

sources.title: Argonautica
sources.description: Comprehensive account of Jason's quest for the Golden Fleece and the adventures of the Argonauts.

sources.title: Theogony
sources.description: Cosmogonic work describing the origins of the gods, the Titanomachy, and the establishment of Olympian rule.

sources.title: Works and Days
sources.description: Didactic poem describing the creation of the world, the five ages of man, and the nature of justice and divine order.

sources.title: Library History
sources.description: Universal history containing numerous Greek myths, legends, and historical accounts from various sources.

sources.title: Description of Greece
sources.description: Comprehensive guide to Greek mythological sites, traditions, and local legends throughout Greece.
